data.title: Ro Khanna's role in Epstein files may be catnip for Democratic voters
data.description:
When Rep. Ro Khanna took to the House floor this week to read aloud the names of six “wealthy, powerful men” whose identities were originally redacted in the Jeffrey Epstein files, it catapulted the congressman further into the national spotlight.

In recent months, Khanna has helped lead the charge around the Epstein case, meeting with victims and pushing for the full release of court documents. In doing so, he’s shifted the political narrative – framing the scandal not just as a saga of abuse but a broader flashpoint of wealth and power. 

Now as Khanna considers whether to run for president in 2028, he is quietly positioning himself as a leading voice in the progressive lane — one that could dominate if Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) decides to run for the Senate instead. 

“It put him on the national stage in a new way and it has cemented his ability to run for president if he wants to,” said Democratic strategist Adam Parkhomenko, who has known Khanna for more than 10 years but acknowledges he hasn’t always agreed with him on policy. 

“It has given him something concrete that he did that he can talk about. He gets to stand up and say he led the effort,” Parkhomenko said. 

As the Epstein story has remained in the news cycle for months, political observers say the congressman not only elevated a deeply real issue by advocating for victims and pushing for transparency. Khanna — who co-chaired Bernie Sanders’s 2020 presidential effort — also cast a light on a system warped by proximity to power and wealth. 

That factor will inevitably score points with the Democratic base which has long viewed the political system as broken. 

“He was on the ground floor of telling this story and linked that to a larger story about this larger construct in politics that permitted it to happen,” said one top Democratic strategist. “That’s really skillful.” 

“He has a lot of claim to being early on that,” the strategist said. 

Khanna hasn’t publicly said if he is considering a White House run, but he has told some allies that he hasn’t ruled anything out. Allies think if the progressive lane is open, he may be inclined to enter the race.

They also think he’ll be able to tout his efforts on the Epstein case; Khanna can argue he teamed up with Republicans including Rep. Thomas Massie (Ky) to tie Speaker Mike Johnson’s (R-La.) hands and force a vote on legislation to release the Epstein files.

“It’s a very compelling story that he can take directly to voters,” the strategist said. “And AOC doesn’t have a story like that to tell.” 

This week, as Attorney General Pam Bondi testified on Capitol Hill before the House Judiciary Committee, Khanna was making the rounds on news shows from CNN’s Anderson Cooper to BBC programming to press the Department of Justice on why so many names were withheld before Congress pushed for the release. 

“This has become a cause for me because I have gotten to know many of the survivors personally,” Khanna told The Hill in a text exchange on Thursday. “They have cried in my office. They text about their anger or hopelessness when they saw Bondi’s testimony. 

“Whatever happens in my future, I will not rest until these survivors have justice,” he said. “I consider it now a duty.”  

Khanna said he began to advocate for the issue “because many of these survivors who were raped came from working class backgrounds. Quite a few didn’t have fathers and the men who raped them were extremely rich and powerful.” 

“The Epstein class represents rich and powerful Americans who believe they are above the law,” the congressman said. “They have shafted the working class, extracted wealth and cheer-led for dumb wars. It’s time to hold the elites accountable.” 

To date, while Khanna is included in 2028 polls, he ranks well below contenders including California Gov. Gavin Newsom and former Vice President Kamala Harris. 

Late last year, in what some called a 2028 preview, he took a swipe at Newsom and said that he has heard a string of gripes from his constituents in the state about “fraud, waste and abuse in Sacramento.” He vowed that there would be congressional hearings on the matter and called for a “full independent audit.” 

The comments rubbed some Democrats the wrong way. 

One Democratic donor said that while Khanna was “smart and thoughtful,” he has “oversaturated his brand” by taking on too much and having too much of a presence on social media and the cable networks. 

“It’s too much,” the donor said. 

But Democratic strategist Joel Payne pushed back on that criticism saying that there’s no such thing as being “omnipresent.” 

“I think that’s too dated,” Payne said. 

Khanna, he said, “has done well going anywhere, talking to anyone and has earned a good reputation for finding issues that resonate outside the bubble.” 

Julian Zelizer, a professor of history and public affairs at Princeton University, said while Khanna’s role in pushing for transparency around the Epstein files has “certainly elevated his profile and demonstrated his skill at forming bipartisan coalitions” to combat President Trump’s efforts, voters may look to him for his ideas on other issues, including the economy. 

“What will be important is that he can return to the affordability and economic nationalism agenda that had been his bread and butter — and which will remain front and center in the coming years,” Zelizer said. 

In the text exchange with The Hill, Khanna said of his efforts around the Epstein files, “ultimately, it’s not just about rhetoric.

“People respect that Massie and I took action,” Khanna said. “We got a bill passed and forced the President to sign it. We forced the release of millions of files. I went to the floor of the House and named names in a speech that has gone viral. 

“We need leaders with the strength of conviction to take on corrupting forces in our democracy and stand for people,” he said.

data.title: Democrats' tariff gambit sparks new headaches for Mike Johnson
data.description:
This week’s rebuke of President Trump’s tariff regime by a small group of House Republicans is only the start of what will likely be a series of headaches over the issue for Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.), threatening to derail the White House’s messaging on the tariffs in a critical election year.

First, Republican leaders failed to get enough support in the razor-thin majority to reinstate a ban on members forcing snap votes to repeal national emergency authority, with three members defecting. Then, six House Republicans sided with Democrats in a Wednesday vote supporting a resolution led by Rep. Gregory Meeks (D-N.Y.) to repeal Trump’s tariffs on Canada.

Now, Democrats are plotting to force a wave of votes to repeal Trump’s Mexico tariffs, Brazil tariffs, “Liberation Day” global tariffs and others, hoping to keep the issue in the headlines, continuously pit Republicans against Trump and sharply criticize vulnerable Republicans who don’t back their bills.

Meeks, the ranking member of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, told reporters on Wednesday he would bring those measures to the floor when the House returns.

“I’ll just say they’re coming,” Meeks warned.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) promised Thursday that Democrats are “going to continue to press the case … to get rid of these Trump tariffs that are raising costs on everyday Americans by thousands of dollars per year.”

Wednesday’s rebellion on the Canada tariff vote by Reps. Don Bacon (R-Neb.), Kevin Kiley (R-Calif.), Dan Newhouse (R-Wash.), Jeff Hurd (R-Colo.), Brian Fitzpatrick (R-Pa.) and Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) underscored the fissures within the Republican Party over Trump’s trade policy and highlighted Johnson’s difficulty in maintaining unity within his razor-thin majority.

Trump, just as the vote was wrapping up, issued a primary threat against those who opposed his tariff regime — potentially dissuading some Republicans running in tight seats from backing Democrats’ tariff resolutions.

“Any Republican, in the House or the Senate, that votes against TARIFFS will seriously suffer the consequences come Election time, and that includes Primaries!” he wrote. 

The bad headlines for Trump won’t end with Wednesday’s vote. The Senate, where Democrats are also eager to open GOP wounds, will be compelled to take up the House’s Canada resolution and any other privileged resolutions it passes. The Canada resolution is likely to pass the Senate, which has twice previously passed a resolution to repeal Canada tariffs, and head to Trump’s desk. 

But the resolutions are unlikely to end in repeal of the tariffs, since Trump will surely veto the measures, which Congress will be unable to override. Johnson and other leaders have sought to convince members that Trump’s certain veto makes the tariff repeal votes a futile effort, and argued that even tariff-skeptical Republicans should wait for a pending Supreme Court decision in a case challenging the legality of the national emergencies behind Trump’s tariffs.

The vote did show GOP leaders that show those arguments could be working — along with fear of wrath from Trump.

The rebellion on the vote to repeal the Canada tariffs was not nearly as big as Republican leaders and the White House feared, or that tariff-skeptical Republicans expected.

One leadership source told The Hill that they were expecting the number of GOP defections to be in the high teens. House Majority Whip Tom Emmer (R-Minn.) was on the phone with members for hours to get them to flip. 

Members who had signaled discontent with tariffs on previous votes — including Reps. Victoria Spartz (R-Ind.), Jay Obernolte (R-Calif.), and Tom McClintock (R-Calif.) — did not vote against the wishes of Trump this time. 

Getting the number of defections down to just six members was seen as a major success, and is raising Republicans’ hopes of narrowing down the GOP rebellions on other tariff votes.

Canada was seen as the repeal resolution that Republicans were most likely to support, due to the USMCA free trade agreement and a number of members being from states that trade heavily with Canada.

Kiley, one of the six defectors, acknowledged that reality: “I think there are some sort of unique issues in play with respect to Canada, you know, for obvious reasons, given the issue of proximity and the existence of a free trade agreement among our countries.”

Meeks said the Canada vote taught him a lot about reaching to members and finding out what is important in their districts — and encouraged members to find courage to stand up against Trump on the tariffs.

“Anytime you have someone to stand up to him on a continuous basis, he backs down. So that’s what has to happen. Don’t be afraid of the bully,” Meeks said. “If you stand up and push back, you’re going to be okay, and the bully will change. And that’s been proven over and over again.”

Newhouse, who represents an agriculture-heavy district that has been hit hard by the tariffs, told The Hill that he would review the other resolutions on a “case-by-case” basis, but added that tariffs have driven up the cost of inputs, equipment and fertilizer for farmers.

“Overall, he has a definite strategy, and I think there’s been a lot of good accomplished that’s actually brought people to the table – other countries. I think, talking to the trade representatives, we’ve seen a lot of progress, because they have more leverage now to be able to negotiate better deals. Long-term tariffs do hurt, and so we need those deals to happen. But it’s a kind of a case by case basis. Like I said, Canada, we have a very close economic relationship,” Newhouse said.

For Democrats’ messaging in the midterms, though, it doesn’t matter too much whether the next tariff repeal votes succeed or fail. Any vulnerable Republicans who does not vote in favor of tariff repeals will be hammered for bowing to Trump on an unpopular policy.

“By voting in favor of price hiking tariffs, vulnerable House Republicans chose fealty to Donald Trump and their Deputy Speaker over addressing the affordability crisis haunting their constituents. There are no more ‘moderates’ left in the Republican Party, just corrupt sycophants,” Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee spokesperson Justin Chermol said in a statement on Thursday.

And Johnson still has an incredibly tough task in securing what amounts to 99.5 percent Republican support on any party-line vote — able to lose no more than one GOP member on any given vote — particularly when GOP leaders at this point are essentially writing off Massie as an automatic “no” vote.

Massie, who was also one of three members to vote down the proposal to ban snap tariff-repeal votes, said Tuesday night that Republican leaders did not even attempt to reach out to him to try to change his mind.

Asked what leadership said to him to try to get him to change his position, Massie said: “Nothing.” Leaders did not try “at all” to sway him, he said.

Bacon, who is not running for reelection and therefore does not need to fear a primary challenge, immediately left the House floor after voting down the ban on snap votes, resisting attempts to try to sway him.

The six Republicans who rebuked Trump on the tariffs said that tariffs have been harming their constituents and that tariff authority needs to be brought back to Congress, which they argue had ceded too much power to the White House.

“If we normalize broad emergency trade powers today, we should expect that a future president—of either party—will rely on the same authority in ways many of us would strongly oppose. Institutional consistency matters. The Constitution does not shift depending on who occupies the White House. My responsibility is to defend the separation of powers regardless of political convenience,” Hurd wrote on X.

Johnson expressed disappointment with members allowing snap tariff repeal votes on Fox Business’s “Mornings with Maria” on Wednesday morning.

“This is life with a razor-thin majority as we have, and sometimes this happens,” Johnson said. 

“I don’t think we need to go down the road of trying to limit the president’s power while he is in the midst of negotiating America First trade agreements with nations around the world,” he added.

data.title: Party balloons? FAA's surprise El Paso airspace closure fuels questions
data.description:
The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) surprise closure and later opening of airspace over El Paso, Texas is fueling questions from the public and sharp rebukes from lawmakers. 

Trump administration officials initially contended the move came in response to the incursion of Mexican drug cartel drones. 

However, the closure of airspace — initially set to last 10 days, but lifted after 8 hours — reportedly came after Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officials in the area used a laser counter-drone weapon, provided by the Pentagon, to take out objects that were later identified as party balloons. 

A White House official told The Hill on Thursday that FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford pulled the trigger on closing the airspace without notifying the White House, DHS and the Pentagon.

“The Department of War and the Department of Transportation having been working together for months regarding drone incursion operations. Last night’s action to disable the cartel drones was not a spontaneous action,” the official said in a statement. “At no point in the process of disabling these cartel drones were civilian aircraft in danger as a result of the methods used by DOW to disable the drones.”

That explanation has not put to bed questions about why the FAA acted alone, why the decision was reversed so quickly, what CBP’s role was in the preceding events, or why officials initially defended the move as a necessary response to “address a cartel drone incursion.”

“The FAA is in charge of the notification and the process of closing the airspace, but it’s historically done in consultation and coordination with other agencies, right? Because other agencies have equities in airspace,” said Charles Marino, ex-advisor to former Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Janet Napolitano. 

Marino, who is now chief executive officer of global security and intelligence advisory firm Sentinel, told The Hill on Thursday that he finds it “weird that the FAA would be the first stop regarding an imminent threat.” 

“Why was everybody out of the loop? Why was everybody caught off guard with this? And so normally, you don’t have the FAA as the initial receiver of a threat,” he said. 

The Associated Press, citing two sources familiar with the situation, reported that the laser was deployed near Fort Bliss without coordinating with the FAA, and despite a meeting scheduled for later this month between the Pentagon and the FAA to discuss safety concerns.

Typically, airspace closures occur during a significant national security event, tied to an event like the Super Bowl or linked to a person traveling, such as the U.S. president. 

The incident appears to be another example of a lack of communication between federal transportation regulators and the military.  

National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) Chair Jennifer Homendy, during a hearing on the deadly crash in Washington last year between a civilian airliner and military helicopter, told lawmakers she was “astonished” but “not surprised” by the El Paso situation. 

“I will say there has been miscommunication, or no communication, between at least the Army and FAA for years now,” Homendy told the Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee on Thursday. 

When reached for comment, the CBP office referred The Hill to the White House. 

Rich Davis, a former chief security officer at United Airlines, said nobody was completely at fault and argued that the FAA likely acted on available intelligence in its initial mitigation response. 

“I believe they felt that this was the proper initial response to ensure safety and security. As in many developing security situations, new information can lead to modifications to first response. And in this case, it was the anti-drone laser,” Davis, now a senior security advisor at International SOS, told The Hill on Thursday. 

The FAA did not respond to The Hill’s request for comment. The FAA administrator was in Congress for a briefing on Wednesday, but declined to answer reporters’ questions upon leaving the building. 

For months, Trump administration officials and some lawmakers have alleged that Mexican drug cartels are using drones along the U.S.-Mexico border to surveil law enforcement and smuggle narcotics. 

In the second half of 2024, U.S. officials tracked about 60,000 drone flights within about a third of a mile of the border, Steven Willoughby, director of the counter-drone program at DHS, said during his congressional testimony in July. During that time, federal authorities confiscated around 1,200 pounds of drugs trafficked via drones, he added. 

Vanda Felbab-Brown, a senior fellow in the Strobe Talbott Center for Security, Strategy, and Technology in the Foreign Policy program at Brookings, said the number of Mexican drug cartel drones intruding into U.S. airspace has risen substantially, posing a growing risk to civilian aircraft.  

Felbab-Brown, who is an expert on non-state armed groups, told The Hill on Thursday that there is a “significant amount” of drones belonging to Mexican drug cartels crossing into U.S. territory, to surveil law enforcement and deliver drug payloads.

The abrupt closure and lack of information around the developments have left lawmakers frustrated. 

Reps. Veronica Escobar (D-Texas), Gabe Vasquez (D-N.M.) and Democratic New Mexico Sens. Martin Heinrich and Ben Ray Luján are asking for a classified briefing on the incident

“Events like this are inexcusable and cause lasting degradation of trust among communities with their local and national leaders. We look forward to the briefing,” lawmakers wrote in a Thursday letter to DHS Secretary Kristi Noem, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy. 

The lawmakers also want to know why a “temporary flight restriction notice” for southern New Mexico remains in place. 

Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas, the chair of the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation, has also called for a classified briefing with Bedford. 

Davis, the former United Airlines security chief, said consequences for El Paso Airport, which had more than 4 million passengers pass through in 2024, were “relatively minimal,” but “certainly disrupted the integrity of the El Paso airport operations.” 

“While the closure was not lengthy, it can still have residual effects,” he added.

data.title: Top lawyer for Goldman Sachs resigns over Epstein documents
data.description:
Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon said Thursday that Kathy Ruemmler, the investment banking company’s top lawyer, will resign after the Department of Justice (DOJ) released its remaining files connected to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, revealing a close relationship between the two.

Ruemmler said she will “step down as Chief Legal Officer and General Counsel of Goldman Sachs as of June 30, 2026,” according to a statement obtained by The Guardian.

“As one of the most accomplished professionals in her field, Kathy has also been a mentor and friend to many of our people, and she will be missed,” Solomon said in a statement obtained by Reuters. “I accepted her resignation, and I respect her decision.”

A Goldman Sachs spokesperson told The Guardian that Ruemmler “regrets ever knowing him.”

“I made the determination that the media attention on me, relating to my prior work as a defense attorney, was becoming a distraction,” she told the Financial Times.

The DOJ’s latest release showed emails where Ruemmler called Epstein “Uncle Jeffrey” in 2018, a decade after the financier pleaded guilty to soliciting sex from teenage girls. Other documents show that she helped Epstein with how to respond to media inquiries about his crimes.

The files also show that Ruemmler and Epstein communicated with each other between 2014 and 2019, including a call Epstein made when he was arrested in July 2019.

Communications with Epstein revealed in the DOJ’s documents caused controversy to erupt across Europe.

Peter Mandelson, the former U.K. ambassador to the U.S. who was sacked by Prime Minister Keir Starmer last September, resigned from the House of Lords last week.

Miroslav Lajčák, a former president of the United Nations General Assembly and an adviser to Slovakian Prime Minister Robert Fico, resigned after the files showed him and Epstein texting about women.

Norway’s ambassador to Jordan and Iraq, Mona Juul, is under review by the country’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs after it was revealed that Epstein in 2014 was invited to a dinner party she and her husband hosted.

The DOJ, having faced pressure and calls for accountability from members of Congress, unredacted more names in the files. This culminated in an explosive hearing between Democrats on the House Judiciary Committee, along with Epstein Files Transparency Act co-sponsor Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), and Attorney General Pam Bondi over the files.

Lawmakers were further outraged after the hearing, accusing the DOJ of spying on them as they reviewed the unredacted using DOJ computers.

data.title: Cardi B jokes about fighting ICE at concert: 'They ain't taking my fans'
data.description:
Rapper Cardi B on Wednesday joked about fighting Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) during a concert in California, saying that federal officers “ain’t taking my fans” — prompting a response from the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

“B—-, if ICE comes in here, we’re going to jump their a–es,” Cardi B said onstage in Coachella Valley after she sang part of the late Tejano musician Selena Quintanilla’s song “Como La Flor.” “I got some bear mace in the back. They ain’t taking my fans, b—-, let’s go!”

DHS replied to Cardi B’s comments on the social platform X, writing, “As long as she doesn’t drug and rob our agents, we’ll consider that an improvement over her past behavior.”

“If we talking about drugs let’s talk about Epstein and friends drugging underage girls to rape them. Why yall don’t wanna talk about the Epstein files?” the rapper clapped back in replying to the X post, referring to the Department of Justice’s (DOJ) files connected to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

Cardi B has been an outspoken critic of President Trump and his administration. She slammed the president after last year’s Super Bowl for damaging her designer shoes while in attendance.

“Now he going to have to … bring my uncle to come back, because my uncle got deported,” she said.

The rapper, born Belcalis Almánzar, was also an outspoken supporter of Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) during his 2020 presidential run. She criticized voters for letting “him down” in 2016. The two conducted an interview in 2019 sharing their admiration for former President Franklin D. Roosevelt. Sanders said “it would be great” if she entered politics.

data.title: Joe Rogan explains his appearance in the Epstein files
data.description:
Podcaster Joe Rogan on the Tuesday episode of his show explained his why his name appeared in the Department of Justice’s (DOJ) last release of files related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

“The Joe Rogan Experience” host told guest Cheryl Hines, the wife of Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., that his name was in the files “for not going.”

“Jeffrey Epstein was trying to meet with me,” Rogan said. “And I was like, what? Like, no thanks. It’s not even a possibility that I would’ve ever went, especially after I Googled him.”

“I was like, ‘What the f— are you talking about?'” he continued. “This was like 2017. One of my guests was trying to get me to meet him. I was like, ‘B—- are you high? Like, what the f— are you talking about?’”

Epstein emailed theoretical physicist Lawrence Krauss in September 2017 after Krauss appeared on Rogan’s show. Epstein asked Krauss if he could introduce him to Rogan. Krauss agreed to do so but later said he did not hear from the podcaster.

Rogan told Hines that he would not have met with Epstein unless he was “a guy who is sucking up to the rich and powerful.”

“Some people get intoxicated by being in a circle of rich and powerful people — even if they don’t have any ambitions of being one of those people,” he said. “They just want to be around them.”

Rogan also criticized the DOJ’s redactions in the files, saying they look “f—ing terrible.”

“It looks terrible,” he continued. “It looks terrible for Trump when he was saying that none of this was real, this is all a hoax. This is not a hoax. Like, did you not know? Maybe he didn’t know, if you want to be charitable, but this is definitely not a hoax. And if you’ve got redacted people’s names and these people aren’t victims, you’re not protecting the victims. So what are you doing?”

Rogan, who endorsed President Trump in 2024 and had him as a guest on his podcast, has been outspoken in his criticism of the administration’s handling of the files and their release. In July 2025, Rogan slammed FBI Director Kash Patel over inconsistencies he described about the files.

Patel hedged on a question Rogan asked on the show the month before about if there were videos from Epstein’s island. The FBI director replied, “Not of what you want.”

“The guy’s saying there’s no tapes, there’s no video,” Rogan said a month later. “That doesn’t make any sense. Everyone knows it doesn’t make any sense.”

The host also accused Trump of “gaslighting” the public over his administration’s handling of the files, saying there’s “a line in the sand.”

The DOJ has unredacted several names in the files after members of Congress reviewed the unredacted files, led by Reps. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) and Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), who both sponsored the Epstein Files Transparency Act.

Massie was the only Republican who, along with Democratic members of the House Judiciary Committee, grilled Attorney General Pam Bondi over the Epstein files during a fiery testimony on Wednesday.

“I think that’s what’s compelled them to produce 3 million documents,” Massie said. “And now they’re claiming that it’s incompetence. But [Bondi’s] defense today is incompetence for why they haven’t given us all the documents they should, why they have over-redacted in the case of co-conspirators and why they failed to redact the names of the victims.”

Massie said names were unredacted because Bondi was caught “red-handed.” Bondi accused the Kentucky Republican of having “Trump derangement syndrome” and called him a “failed politician.”

data.title: Bipartisan push in U.S. House to create new visa program, help home builders who rely on immigrant workers
data.description:
AUSTIN (Nexstar) — A Republican congresswoman from Texas said she believes it is now the time for the federal government to pivot its focus from immigration enforcement to immigration reform.

Rep. Monica De La Cruz, R – Texas, is championing a proposal to create a new visa program targeted specifically for construction workers. The proposed visa program is in response to a problem many south Texas builders are experiencing: a lack of immigrant workers.

“Now, it is important that we look at a conversation now that the border is secure under President Trump on ways of reforming the immigration system,” De La Cruz told Nexstar.

De La Cruz said she met with White House officials and Speaker of the House Mike Johnson earlier this week to discuss immigration reform policies. De La Cruz said Johnson is taking all of the thoughts and concerns into consideration, but some south Texas community members say they were not happy with the outcome of the meeting.

What’s happening at the job sites?

Mario Guerrero, the executive director of the South Texas Builders Association, said construction sites are seeing fewer workers because of immigration enforcement. Guerrero said those workers are being targeted by immigration officials at the job site. Some are arrested and deported while others are scared to show up.

Guerrero said Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents are notorious for following cement trucks to job sites in the area and arresting workers as they are in the middle of pouring the foundation.

“So you’re looking at a $30,000 negative loss, off the bat, on a construction site,” Guerrero explained.

Guerrero said there is not a workforce in the local population that fills builders’ needs.

“We don’t have a sideline for workers and it’s not like you could just get somebody off of a couch to start framing a house,” Guerrero explained.

The lack of workers is leading to increased time and expense on building homes. For example, Guerrero said a standard 2,000 square-foot home usually would take crews about four months to build. That time has more than doubled to nine months, Guerrero said.

“What’s gonna happen to the economy if we have builders that start defaulting on their loans,” Guerrero questioned.

He and other south Texas builders have been speaking with local, state, and federal leaders about their concerns. A group of them traveled to Washington D.C. earlier in the month to meet with lawmakers to discuss their issues.

The H-2C visa

De La Cruz is championing the Essential Workers for Economic Advancement Act as a way to alleviate some of the concerns for builders. She is among a bipartisan group of congressional members co-sponsoring the bill that was filed back in September. The bill was filed by Rep. Lloyd Smucker, a Pennsylvania Republican.

“That would help us protect and get immigrants legally for those in the construction industry,” De La Cruz told Nexstar.

The bill would create a new visa program known as the H-2C. A layout of the bill said it would initially make 65,000 visas open to foreigners who will work in construction jobs. Eligible individuals would have to have a job offer and pass a criminal background check. The visa would allow workers to stay in the country for three years with the ability to renew the visa twice. In total, the most an individual could stay in the country is nine years.

Employers would not be allowed to layoff American workers or underpay the temporary workers under the bill’s current language.

Guerrero said he has not given the proposal his endorsement just yet because he feels it will not be an immediate fix to the financial issue some builders are facing.

“If it ever gets to the Senate, the Senate’s gonna make their changes, send it back, and it’s gonna be a long shot before anything happens,” Guerrero said.

The bill was filed back in September and has not had a committee hearing to date.

The meeting with leadership

De La Cruz said she discussed the bill proposal with Johnson this week. Guerrero said one of the board members for the South Texas Builders Association was at the meeting as well and felt it was a waste of time.

“He said that nothing got done and it was just all photo-op,” Guerrero said. He added that the board member felt that he was “censored” and could not fully speak about his concerns and ideas on the issue of immigration enforcement in his community.

Nexstar asked De La Cruz’s office about how Guerrero and the other board member painted the meeting. In a statement, De La Cruz said, “Speaker Johnson and White House officials are engaged and have heard our concerns for common-sense policies to bridge gaps in our immigration system and protect the American dream. On behalf of South Texas, I am fighting to deliver solutions to ensure our South Texas communities remain prosperous.”

data.title: FBI ups reward to $100K in Nancy Guthrie search
data.description:
The FBI on Thursday raised its reward for information leading investigators to Nancy Guthrie, the mother of NBC’s “Today” host Savannah Guthrie, to $100,000 amid the agency’s search near Tucson, Ariz.

The agency also revealed new identifying information about a possible suspect in connection with her disappearance, which investigators have ruled was a kidnapping. The information was based on video recovered from Nancy Guthrie’s Nest doorbell camera from the night she disappeared.

“The suspect is described as a male, approximately 5’9” – 5’10” tall, with an average build,” the FBI’s Phoenix office said in a post on the social platform X. “In the video, he is wearing a black, 25-liter ‘Ozark Trail Hiker Pack’ backpack.”

“We hope this updated description will help concentrate the public tips we are receiving,” the post reads. “Since February 1, 2026, the FBI has collected over 13,000 tips from the public related to this case.”

Investigators recovered multiple pairs of black gloves near Nancy Guthrie’s home that will be run through a DNA analysis, local outlet KVOA reported.

Earlier Thursday, Savannah Guthrie posted an old home video on Instagram giving tribute to her mother. The “Today” host has posted on social media several times asking for help in Nancy’s search and called on her kidnappers to show “proof of life.”

“Our lovely mom,” Savannah Guthrie wrote in her latest post attached to the video. “We will never give up on her. Thank you for your prayers and hope.”

Along with the gloves and footage, TMZ founder Harvey Levin told Fox News on Wednesday that he received a “bizarre” email from someone who claims they know the kidnapper. Levin said the emailer tried to contact Savannah Guthrie’s brother and sister “to no avail.”

“And they said they want one bitcoin sent to a bitcoin address that we have confirmed is active,” Levin added. “It’s a real bitcoin address. And as they put it, time is more than relevant. So, we have no idea whether this is real or not, but they are making a demand.”

Levin also told CNN that there was “activity” from the bitcoin address before he went on air.

FBI investigators said they were “conducting an extensive search” of roadways along Arizona’s Catalina Foothills. No suspects have been identified, but FBI Director Kash Patel said they were looking into “persons of interest.”

data.title: Confusion reigns over Trump meeting with governors
data.description:
A series of contradictions and reversals from the White House and National Governors Association (NGA) this week has bred widespread confusion over the fate of the bipartisan group’s annual meeting with the president.

All governors are expected to gather Feb. 19-21 in Washington, D.C., for the NGA’s annual conference, which has traditionally coincided with a meeting with the president and a separate black-tie dinner at the White House.

The weekend has historically provided a unique opportunity for bipartisanship, but that prospect now appears at risk of unraveling as President Trump continues to give mixed signals about whether he plans to exclude Democratic governors from next weekend’s events.

The president seemed to reverse course Wednesday after his initial decision to invite only Republican governors to the White House compelled Oklahoma Gov. Kevin Stitt (R), chair of the NGA, to cancel the event.

“NGA staff was informed that the White House intends to limit invitations to the annual business meeting, scheduled for February 20, to Republican governors only,” Stitt wrote in a letter to governors early Monday. “Because NGA’s mission is to represent all 55 governors, the Association is no longer serving as the facilitator for that event, and it is no longer included in our official program.”

“We cannot allow one divisive action to achieve its goal of dividing us,” he added. “The solution is not to respond in kind, but to rise above and to remain focused on our shared duty to the people we serve.”

Stitt said in the letter that, at that point, no invitations had gone out yet. A person familiar with the NGA winter meeting planning told The Hill that Republican governors received invitations to the meeting later that day.

After talking to the president on Wednesday, Stitt said Trump backed down from his decision to exclude Democrats from the business meeting next week, attributing the apparent snub earlier to a “misunderstanding in scheduling.”

“He was very clear in his communications with me that this is a National Governors Association’s event, and he looks forward to hosting you and hearing from governors across the country. President Trump said this was always his intention, and we have addressed the misunderstanding in scheduling,” Stitt wrote in a subsequent letter to his fellow governors at 2 p.m. Wednesday. 

“I look forward to seeing each of you in Washington DC next week,” Stitt continued.

Around 30 minutes later, all Democratic governors received their invitations to the Friday meeting at the White House, according to a person with knowledge of the planning.

Earlier reporting indicated that all but two Democratic governors — Maryland Gov. Wes Moore and Colorado Gov. Jared Polis — were invited to the separate White House dinner for governors and their families on Feb. 21.

It’s unclear if Stitt discussed the Feb. 21 dinner with Trump during their phone call Wednesday, but Stitt’s follow-up letter to his fellow governors only mentioned the Friday business meeting.

So, when Trump took to social media later Wednesday — appearing to conflate the business meeting and the lavish dinner, as he lashed out at Stitt — some Democrats and political observers grew confused about the president’s intentions.

Referring to Stitt’s Monday letter to governors, Trump wrote on social media later Wednesday afternoon that the NGA chair “incorrectly stated my position on the very exclusive Governors Annual Dinner and Meeting at the White House.”

Trump quoted the letter as saying the White House intended to “limit invitations to the annual business meeting, scheduled for February 20th, to Republican Governors only.” 

“That is false!” Trump added. “The invitations were sent to ALL Governors, other than two, who I feel are not worthy of being there.”

Trump named Polis and Moore as the two governors he excluded from the invite list, apparently conflating the business meeting and the dinner, to which all Democratic governors except Polis and Moore were invited. 

Trump, in his post, railed against several Democratic governors and accused Stitt of being a “RINO,” or “Republican in Name Only.” That left some to question whether the president intended to invite Democrats.

“Following that invitation, the president made his derogatory and disrespectful Truth Social post, and I believe that has led to a lack of clarity amongst everyone involved,” a person involved in the NGA planning told The Hill.

A spokesperson for Moore’s office confirmed the governor, on Wednesday afternoon, received the invitation to the White House business meeting on Feb. 20. The spokesperson said the governor has not been invited to the dinner. Polis’s office did not respond to a request for comment.

Trump, in a subsequent post Thursday morning, again lashed out at Stitt, calling him a “RINO” and issuing a veiled threat to endorse a primary challenger. The president, in this most recent post, only referred to attendance at the dinner, not the business meeting.

“In any event, we will soon have a Governor in Oklahoma who knows how to accurately write a Press Release to the Public, in this case, to state that I invited, not happily, almost all Democrat Governors to the Governor’s Dinner at the White House. Stitt, a wiseguy, knew this, but tried to get some cheap publicity by stating otherwise,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

“Anyway, I’ll see whoever shows up at the White House, the fewer the better!” he added.

Questions remain about how the NGA and the Democratic Governors Association (DGA) will respond. 

An NGA spokesperson told The Hill on Thursday that the “NGA is not currently facilitating the dinner unless all governors are invited” but that the association has not received any updates so far about the dinner. 

In a statement provided by a spokesperson, Stitt did not address the apparent contradiction, nor did he comment on the exclusion of Polis and Moore from the Feb. 21 dinner. He also did not respond to Trump’s harsh criticism.

“I am glad the White House changed course and decided to invite all the governors to the Feb. 20 business meeting,” Stitt said in an emailed statement.

The DGA had earlier issued a statement refusing to attend any of the NGA events at the White House if not every governor was invited. The Democratic group did not respond to questions about whether the governors would attend the Friday meeting now that all Democrats had secured the invite.

“If the reports are true that not all governors are invited to these events, which have historically been productive and bipartisan opportunities for collaboration, we will not be attending the White House dinner this year. Democratic governors remain united and will never stop fighting to protect and make life better for people in our states,” the group wrote in a statement Thursday.

data.title: Venezuela's Delcy Rodríguez: Maduro still 'legitimate president'
data.description:
Venezuela’s acting President Delcy Rodriguez on Thursday said Nicolás Maduro is still the “legitimate president” following his capture and arrest by the Trump administration. 

“Yes, I can tell you that President Nicolás Maduro is the legitimate president. I can, I will tell you this as the lawyer that I am. They are both, President Maduro and Cilia Flores the first lady, are both innocent,” Rodríguez said during a Thursday appearance on NBC News’s “Meet the Press.”

Maduro is currently facing charges for narcoterrorism, drug trafficking and corruption after serving at the helm of Venezuela’s government operations for years. 

He is currently being held in New York and awaiting trial before a judge. The regime leader pleaded not guilty to all charges, despite pressure from the Department of Justice to admit responsibility for years of drug trafficking throughout the Latin American nation.

Officials in both Venezuela and Bolivia have been accused of transporting illegal narcotics internationally with the help of high-ranking political leaders known as the Cartel of the Suns.

Maduro is accused of providing military-grade weapons to the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC) and coordinating drug routes with groups in Honduras.

While awaiting the regime leader’s fate, Rodriguez has led the country in the midst of a transitional period in which the Trump administration is looking to tap into Venezuela’s oil rich land.

Energy Secretary Chris Wright told NBC that the relationship with Rodriguez is off to a “tremendous start.”

“We’ve been dealing with Delcy for five weeks now. It’s been an amazing cooperation. She’s delivered information,” he told NBC News’s Kristen Welker.

“Everything we know so far has turned out to be true. She’s made enormous positive changes, including already changing the hydrocarbon law in the country in the first few weeks. So, I would say that cooperation is off to a tremendous start,” he added.
